nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically observed hovering several meters above steep outer-reef slopes and isolated coral heads exposed to currents, feeding on drifting zooplankton at depths of roughly 10–132 m

decorPreferences: open swimming space above the rockwork

diet.notes: Atypical among hawkfishes — generally considered primarily a zooplanktivore that has been observed hovering in midwater snapping at drifting copepods, crustacean larvae, and other small planktonic prey. In captivity it has been reported to readily accept a varied carnivore diet of frozen mysis, enriched brine, finely chopped marine meats (shrimp, clam, squid), and quality pellets, ideally offered in small portions multiple times per day to mimic continuous grazing on plankton. Pigment-rich foods (with carotenoids such as krill or paprika-enriched preparations) are generally advised to help maintain its red-orange coloration, which has been reported to fade in captivity on a monotonous diet.

avoidWith: extremely shy or sedentary species that compete for hovering space

summary: An unusual midwater hawkfish from the Indo-West Pacific with a forked lyre-shaped tail; generally considered one of the most peaceful and reef-friendly hawkfishes, hovering above the rockwork to pick zooplankton from the current.
careNotes:
The Red Hawkfish (Cyprinocirrhites polyactis), also widely traded as Lyretail, Swallowtail, Soaring, or Pink Hawkfish, is the standout exception within the family Cirrhitidae. Where most hawkfishes are bottom-perching ambush predators, this species has been observed hovering several meters above the substrate in the wild, snapping at drifting zooplankton — a habit reflected in its larger pectoral fins, deeply forked lunate caudal fin, and unusually extended first dorsal ray. It does not have a swim bladder, so the hovering is an energetic behaviour punctuated by brief rests on rocks or sand.

In the aquarium it is generally considered one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly hawkfishes, and is one of the few hawkfishes commonly described as suitable for reef tanks. Reef compatibility is most often rated 'with caution': mobile ornamental crustaceans such as small cleaner shrimp and tiny crabs may be picked off, but corals, anemones, and larger shrimp are typically left alone. Temperament across sources spans from 'peaceful' (BlueZoo Aquatics) to 'semi-aggressive' (LiveAquaria, reefapp); it is widely reported as territorial toward other hawkfish but tolerant of unrelated tankmates, so housing as a single fish or as a simultaneously-introduced bonded pair is generally advised. As with other Cirrhitidae it is widely understood to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite capable of changing sex under social cues, which is what allows captive pairing.

Unlike most hawkfishes, this species needs ample open swimming room above the rockwork in addition to ledges and crevices to perch on. Moderate to brisk laminar flow that lets it hover and pick at suspended food is generally advised. Multiple small feedings per day are recommended over one large feeding because of its planktivorous habit. A tightly-sealed lid is sensible given the family's general tendency to jump when startled, though this species is less prone to jumping than the strictly benthic hawkfishes.

Cross-source spread: Adult size — LiveAquaria 6 in (15.2 cm), BlueZoo 5 in (12.7 cm), FishBase 15 cm TL, reefapp 15 cm; averaged to ~13–15 cm. Temperature — LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase wild observations 20–28.3°C (mean 26.8°C); averaged to a hobby-realistic 23–27°C. Salinity — LiveAquaria 1.020–1.025 SG (no other source quantified it); retained as 1.020–1.025. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria and BlueZoo 30 US gal (~114 L), reefapp 150 L; biased upward to 150 L per the tank-size special rule given the species' need for ample midwater swimming space and active hovering behaviour. Temperament — BlueZoo described as 'peaceful' / 'graceful' while LiveAquaria, reefapp and AquariumDomain rated 'semi-aggressive'; consensus pick semi-aggressive with the caveat that it is among the least aggressive hawkfishes. Reef-safe — BlueZoo 'yes', LiveAquaria and reefapp 'with caution' due to risk to small crustaceans; consensus 'with caution'.

Secondary source rotation: the originally-designated AquariumDomain page returned HTTP 404, so FishBase and reefapp.net were used as additional secondaries (plus a confirmatory Reef Life Survey / Australian Museum search via WebSearch for behavioural detail).
breedingNotes: Has not been widely reported as captive-bred in home aquaria. As with other Cirrhitidae it is generally understood to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite; pairs that form in captivity have been documented to court at dusk, releasing pelagic eggs into the current. Larval rearing is considered difficult because of an extended planktonic phase, so successful aquaculture remains uncommon.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page (linked from /collections/hawkfish). Provided scientific name (Cyprinocirrhites polyactis), family (Cirrhitidae), max size 6 in (15.2 cm), minimum tank 30 gal, temperament Semi-Aggressive, reef-safe With Caution, care level Easy, diet (variety of marine meats, frozen preparations, live feeder shrimp), and water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12). Origin listed as Maldives, Indonesia, New Caledonia.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Cross-checked common names (Lyretail, Swallowtail, Soaring, Pink Hawkfish), max size 5 in, minimum tank 30 gal, temperament described as semi-aggressive but 'peaceful', reef-safe yes, care level beginner, diet primarily zooplankton — and the key behavioural note that this species hovers above live rock and coral picking food from the water column, distinguishing it from other hawkfishes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (family Cirrhitidae, order Centrarchiformes, authority Bleeker 1874), max length 15 cm TL, depth 10–132 m, environment marine reef-associated, climate tropical, wild temperature 20–28.3°C (mean 26.8°C), native range East Africa to Tonga and Japan to New Zealand, habitat (steep slopes / small coral heads exposed to currents, hovers above seafloor feeding on planktonic crustaceans and copepods), trophic level 3.2 (carnivore/planktivore), IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Additional cross-check (substituted because the originally-tried AquariumDomain page 404'd). Max size 15 cm, minimum tank 150 L, semi-aggressive (especially toward similar-looking fish or late additions), reef-safe with caution due to threat to small crustaceans, can be kept as a simultaneously-introduced pair, color-fade in captivity without pigmented food, capable of female-to-male sex change.

additional.name: Reef Life Survey / Australian Museum (via web search)
additional.notes: Behavioural confirmation only: this species hovers metres above the seabed snapping at zooplankton, in contrast to bottom-squatting congeners, and lacks a swim bladder so it alternates between active hovering and brief rests. Used to justify the 'mid' swim zone and the open-swimming-space tank-decor preference.
